coredata.dc:title: Efficient cleavage of C-C bond in lignin adopting Z-scheme heterojunction photocatalyst by g-C3N4/CQDs/WO3

coredata.dc:description: It is a practical approach to overcome the shortage of energy and environmental pollution simultaneously if obtaining useful products from biomass through chemical decomposition. Photocatalytic depolymerization of lignin to generate high value-added chemicals has received extensive attention for decades. The low yield of aromatic monomers is still one of the urgent problems to be solved. Selective breakage of C α -C β bonds in lignin is of importance to improve the yield of aromatic monomers. Herein, Z-scheme heterojunction g-C3N4/CQDs/WO3 was constructed by combining graphite-like carbon nitride (g-C3N4), tungsten oxide (WO3) with carbon quantum dots (CQDs) to break the C-C bond selectively in β-O-4 model compounds. Both the efficiencies of substrate conversion and selectivity of C-C bond cleavage reached to 99 %. As a result, the total yield of benzaldehyde and benzoic acid attained to 83.92 %. Furthermore, the photocatalyst has satisfactory effect on depolymerization of four types of lignin, while the heterojunction can validly reduce the recombination rate of photogenerated carriers. Additionally, CQDs broadens the light absorption range and enhances the mobility of photogenerated carriers. Consequently, this study provides a feasible idea for highly selective cleavage of lignin C-C bonds through heterojunction engineering.

head.ce:title: Efficient cleavage of C-C bond in lignin adopting Z-scheme heterojunction photocatalyst by g-C3N4/CQDs/WO3

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: It is a practical approach to overcome the shortage of energy and environmental pollution simultaneously if obtaining useful products from biomass through chemical decomposition. Photocatalytic depolymerization of lignin to generate high value-added chemicals has received extensive attention for decades. The low yield of aromatic monomers is still one of the urgent problems to be solved. Selective breakage of C α -C β bonds in lignin is of importance to improve the yield of aromatic monomers. Herein, Z-scheme heterojunction g-C3N4/CQDs/WO3 was constructed by combining graphite-like carbon nitride (g-C3N4), tungsten oxide (WO3) with carbon quantum dots (CQDs) to break the C-C bond selectively in β-O-4 model compounds. Both the efficiencies of substrate conversion and selectivity of C-C bond cleavage reached to 99 %. As a result, the total yield of benzaldehyde and benzoic acid attained to 83.92 %. Furthermore, the photocatalyst has satisfactory effect on depolymerization of four types of lignin, while the heterojunction can validly reduce the recombination rate of photogenerated carriers. Additionally, CQDs broadens the light absorption range and enhances the mobility of photogenerated carriers. Consequently, this study provides a feasible idea for highly selective cleavage of lignin C-C bonds through heterojunction engineering.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Synopsis. This study provides a strategy to selectively cleavage the C-C bond in lignin by Z-scheme heterojunction, which renders a new idea for the high-value utilization of lignin.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Lignocellulose is namely one of the most abundant renewable resources in nature [1], whose basic component, lignin, is an ideal raw material for the production of aromatic chemicals [2]. However, lignin is hard to depolymerize due to its unique structure, which makes it usually discarded as debris as well. This not only is wastes of resources, but also causes serious environmental issues [3]. In the past few decades, lignin has been developed to possess added value by means of pyrolysis [4–6], oxidation [7,8] and reduction depolymerization [9,10] in order to handle the resource and environmental problems aroused by lignin. Harsh reaction conditions of depolymerization are an unsustainable strategy, even though large quantities of aromatic mixtures can be obtained.

Photocatalysis has been widely applied in the field of biomass conversion owing to its mild reaction conditions, low energy consumption and high selectivity [11,12] in recent years. Photogenerated carriers and active intermediates are the crucial factors for splitting lignin linkages for photocatalytic depolymerization of lignin. It has also received extensive attention because the content of β-O-4 bond accounts for approximately half of the natural lignin linkages [13]. The dissociation energies of C α -C β and C β -O bonds in the β-O-4 bond structure are different, and most studies focus on C β -O bonds for the reason of its lower dissociation energies [14]. Liu et al. [15] broke selectively the C-O bond in lignin by the S-scheme heterojunction g-C3N4/Zn4In2S7 with the selectivity reaching to 99 %, whose yields of phenol and acetophenone were achieved to 93.4 % and 75.2 %, respectively. The yield of aromatic monomers is hard to attain 50 % yet [16], although the selective cleavage of C-O bond has been greatly developed. Therefore, the cleavage of C-C bond is an ideal solution to improve the yield of aromatic monomers. Diverse homogeneous catalysts were reported, for instance, metal ion complexes (vanadium, iridium, or cerium, etc.) [17–19], polyoxometalate [20], and metal-free organic catalysts [21,22], which indicate a desirable effect on the cleavage of C-C bond, but the catalysts are tough to be separated and recovered from the homogeneous systems. TiO2 in a micellar aqueous solution by Xu et al. [23] could catalyze the cleavage of β-1 type model compounds, with the main products of benzaldehyde (21.9 %), benzoic acid (62.8 %) and 1,2-diphenylethanone (12.9 %). Polyoxometalate-modified TiO2 was proposed to further explore the effect of heterogeneous catalysts on the cleavage of C-C bonds in the β-O-4 bond structure. The conversion of 2-phenoxy-1-phenylethanol (PP-ol) amounted to 99 % with the main products as benzaldehyde and phenol [24]. As a result, heterogeneous catalysts affect considerably on C-C bond cleavage, nonetheless the selectivity of C-C bond cleavage is still a critical problem to be solved.

Recently organic semiconductor-like graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4) is widely employed in visible-light-driven photocatalytic energy conversion, chemical substance synthesis, and environmental remediation because of its narrow band gap, simple preparation process, and good stability [25]. g-C3N4 has attracted researchers’ attention in the field of photocatalysis since Wang et al. [26] firstly applied g-C3N4 to the field of photocatalytic hydrogen production. Anagnostopoulou et al. [27] constructed Co3O4/g-C3N4 heterojunctions and employed them into photocatalytic hydrogen production and CO2 reduction. In addition, Liu et al. [28] successfully broke the C α -C β bond by π-π stacking interaction of mesoporous graphitic carbon nitride to scale up the conversion to 96 % and C-C bond selectivity of lignin model compounds to 91 %, respectively. Deng et al. [16] fabricated a single catalyst of g-C3N4 loaded with Ru atoms to furtherly improve the C-C bond cleavage efficiency of graphitic carbon nitride. The C-C bond cleavage selectivity amounted to 98 %, and the substrate conversion rate achieved to 99 %. The construction of heterojunctions is a more effective solution for the purpose of reducing the complexation rate of photogenerated carriers in a single catalyst.

WO3 has become a popular semiconductor photocatalyst for its strong oxidation ability, suitable band gap and good light corrosion resistance [29]. The g-C3N4/WO3 heterojunction can effectively reduce not only the recombination rate of photogenerated carriers, but also enhance the redox ability of the photocatalyst. The study on WO3/g-C3N4 heterojunction has been applied in the field of hydrogen production [30,31] and degradation of pollutants [32,33]. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), a zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterial, possessed the property of up-conversion luminescence (UCPL) and behavior as an electronic medium. While the hydroxyl and carboxyl groups of CQDs are able to act as nucleation sites to form strong interfacial bonds with other semiconductors [34,35]. The construction of g-C3N4/CQDs/WO3 Z-scheme heterojunction can not only respond to visible light, reduce the recombination rate of photogenerated carriers, but also improve the mobility of photocarriers. Additionally, it is also a novel strategy to apply it to the field of lignin depolymerization.

In this study, the Z-scheme heterojunction g-C3N4/CQDs/WO3 was constructed by ultrasonic wet assisted impregnation method, whose structure, morphology and optical properties of the photocatalyst were verified by a series of characterization methods. Lignin and its model compounds were utilized as reaction substrates to evaluate the photocatalytic performance of the catalysts. Meanwhile, the photocatalytic mechanism was further explored by free radical capture experiments and electron spin resonance (ESR).

Various g-C3N4/CQDs/WO3 were fabricated by ultrasonic assisted wet impregnation method [36]. Normally, 5 mL of CQDs solution, different amounts of WO3, and g-C3N4 (CN) were added into 5 mL of deionized water then sonicated for 1 h. Then the mixture was agitated at room temperature for 24 h, centrifuged, and washed by H2O/C2H5OH, and finally dried at 60 °C for 6 h afterwards to obtain different g-C3N4/CQDs/WO3 photocatalysts. Accordingly, at optimal inputs of CN and WO3 (0.75 g CN and 0.05 g WO3), 0 mL, 1 mL, 10 mL, and 15 mL of CQDs solution were added followed by the same procedure as above. The prepared g-C3N4/CQDs/WO3 nomenclature is shown in Table S2.

Lignin model compounds were prepared and lignin extracted as shown in SI-1, and the standard curves are depicted in Fig. S1 and Table S1. Materials, preparation methods, characterization apparatus, computational methods and photocatalytic conversion experiments of CN, CQDs and WO3 are presented in SI-2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Composition and morphology of the photocatalysts
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 1a shows the X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of the catalysts. The diffraction peaks at 13.18° and 27.68° are attributed to the (100) and (002) crystal planes of the original g-C3N4 (JCPDS No. 87–1526), respectively [31]. The (002) crystal plane corresponds to the basic structure with alternating C and N. The (100) crystal plane represents the triazine ring structure of CN [37]. The main peak position is obviously positively shifted compared with the original CN, which is the change of the interlayer spacing caused by the shrinkage of the CN layer [38]. The pattern of WO3 is indexed as an orthorhombic crystal system (JCPDS No. 20–1324). All the diffraction peaks of WO3 appeared in the XRD spectra of both 0.6CW and 0.6CCW. Besides, the (002) crystallographic diffraction peak of CN at 27.86˚ occurred. Due to the low intensity of the CN diffraction peak at 13.18 °, it is difficult to be detected on the XRD pattern of the composite [39]. For all that, it is not hard to see from the figure that the patterns of 0.6CW and 0.6CCW are almost the same, which is aroused by the low content of CQDs [40].

The functional group information of the catalyst was studied by Fourier transform infrared spectra (FTIR). As shown in Fig. 1b, the broad peak at 945–570 cm−1 is related to the tensile vibrations of W-O-W and O-W-O of WO3 [32]. In the FTIR spectra of CQDs, the absorption peak at 1404 cm−1 is attributed to the hydroxyl functional group [41]. And absorption peaks at 1556 cm−1 and 1650 cm−1 associated with CC and CO stretching vibrations [42]. The absorption peaks at 810 cm−1 and 887 cm−1 correspond to the triazine ring respiration mode of CN and the in-plane bending vibration of N-H, respectively [43,44]. Moreover, the bending and stretching vibrations of the C-N heterocycle resulted in absorption peaks in the range of 1600–1200 cm−1 [45]. Notably, the broad absorption peak at 3660–3000 cm−1 is related to the N-H stretching vibration of CN and the O-H stretching vibration of adsorbed water [46]. In addition, the wide absorption band strength of the composites at 1000–500 cm−1 is significantly improved, and all of them show the characteristic peaks of CN. This hints that both WO3 and CN are present in the composites.

The morphology and microstructure of CN, WO3, 0.6CCW are shown in Fig. 2. The scanning electron microscopy (SEM) image (Fig. 2a) presented CN with irregular folded porous lamellar structure. The irregular bulk accumulation morphology of WO3 is shown in Fig. 2b. For 0.6 CCW, the SEM image (Fig. 2c) manifests that WO3 is dispersed on the CN surface, which is further corroborated by its TEM image (Fig. 2d). Furthermore, the lattice fringes of CN and WO3 can be clearly observed from the HRTEM image of 0.6CCW (Fig. 2e). The stripe spacing of 0.337 nm corresponds to the (002) crystal plane of hexagonal CN. The stripe spacing of 0.330 nm, 0.372 nm and 0.382 nm are indexed to the (120), (020) and (001) crystal planes of orthogonal WO3, respectively [38]. In order to further determine the elemental composition of the catalyst, an Energy Dispersive Spectrometer (EDS) test was performed on 0.6CCW. Fig. 2f and Fig. S2 demonstrate the elemental distribution of C, N, O, and W, indicating the presence of all four of these elements in 0.6 CCW.

CN, WO3 and 0.6CCW X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra are showcased in Fig. 3. XPS full spectrum (Fig. 3a) exhibits the four elements C, N, O, W of the catalyst. It can be seen from the C1s high-resolution spectrum of Fig. 3b that CN and 0.6CCW have three similar peaks. The peaks at 287.36 eV and 292.46 eV attribute to the N-C-N and N-CN bonds of CN, respectively [47]. The peak at 283.97 eV is associated with graphitic or amorphous carbon, and the introduction of CQDs results in a significant enhancement of the peak intensity [31,48]. All three peaks of 0.6CCW are shifted to higher binding energies compared to CN, which is caused by the charge transfer at the interface between CN and WO3 [30]. Fig. 3c is the N1s spectra of CN and 0.6CCW. The binding energies of CN at 397.77 eV, 398.95 eV and 399.99 eV are assigned to sp2 hybridized CN-C, sp3 hybridized N-(C)3 and sp3 hybridized C-NH2, respectively [49]. Besides, the peak at 403.18 eV is induced by π excitation [31]. The conjugate effect between CN and WO3 makes the characteristic peak of 0.6CCW shift positively and the π excitation peak enhance [50]. The O1s spectrum of WO3 (Fig. 3d) reflects two peaks located at 529.3 eV and 531.47 eV, belonging to W-O-W and adsorbed oxygen, respectively [37]. In the O1s spectrum at 0.6 CCW, the adsorbed water produces a characteristic peak at 532.25 eV, in addition to the characteristic peaks for W-O-W (529.18 eV) and adsorbed oxygen (531.26 eV) [51]. The slight negative shift of the characteristic peak is attributed to the flow of CN electron cloud to WO3, which leads to increase of the density in electron cloud of WO3, thereby reducing the binding energy of W-O-W and further ability of adsorbing oxygen [44]. Furthermore, the addition of CN resulted in an increase in the intensity of the adsorbed oxygen peak at 531.26. The W4f spectrum is displayed in Fig. 3e. The W4f spectrum of WO3 exhibits three characteristic peaks of W4f7/2 (34.48 eV), W4f5/2 (36.67 eV) and W5p3/2 (40.09 eV). And the W4f characteristic peak of 0.6CCW shifts to the direction of low binding energy, which is also attributed to charge transfer [30]. The above analytical results demonstrate that CN, CQDs and WO3 form a more stable heterojunction structure.

The Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) method was used to study the specific surface area of CN, WO3 and 0.6CCW. Fig. 3f and Fig. 3f illustrations are the N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms and hole-size distribution curves of the three catalysts, respectively. It is not hard to see that the three isotherms are type IV isotherms, indicating that the catalysts are a mesoporous material [47]. The average pore size distribution curve further confirmed the mesoporous structure of the material. Table 1 lists the specific surface area, pore volume and average pore size data of the three catalysts. 0.6CCW has a larger specific surface area and pore volume as well as a smaller average pore size distribution than a single material, which increases the contact sites with the substrate and promotes the reaction [31]. The analytical results correspond to the XPS spectra.

Fig. 4a shows the UV-Vis DRS spectra of the photocatalyst. The optical absorption edges of CN and WO3 are about 433.1 nm and 489.2 nm, respectively. As for 0.6CW, the optical absorption edge is located at 455.7 nm between CN and WO3. The light absorption edge (466.5 nm) of 0.6CCW is red-shifted, which expands the light absorption range due to the UCPL properties of CQDs [52]. PL spectra can measure the recombination rate of photogenerated carriers [53]. As shown in Fig. 4b, the emission peaks around 700 nm were obtained at an excitation wavelength of 350 nm. The peak intensities of CN and WO3 are much higher than those of the composite catalysts, which proves that they have stronger photogenerated carrier recombination rate. The reason for the lower emission peak intensity of 0.6CCW compared to 0.6CW may be that the CQDs act as electronic mediators, which promotes the transfer of electrons and improves the separation rate of photogenerated carriers. The mobility of photogenerated carriers was evaluated by EIS. As for Fig. 4c, it is easy to see that the arc radius of CN and WO3 is larger than that of the composite catalyst, which indicates that the construction of heterojunctions can significantly reduce the internal resistance of the materials and further improve the mobility of photogenerated carriers [54]. CQDs as electronic mediators are able to promote photogenerated carrier migration, which leads to a 0.6CCW circular radius smaller than 0.6CW. The transient photocurrent response spectrum (Fig. 4d) can be used to analyze the mobility and recombination rate of photogenerated carriers [55]. Among all the catalysts, the photocurrent density of 0.6CCW is the highest (∼3.5 mA/cm2), which is 7 times that of CN (∼0.5 mA/cm2), 3 times that of WO3 (∼1.3 mA/cm2) and 2 times that of 0.6CW (∼1.5 mA/cm2). It can be seen that it has higher separation efficiency of photogenerated carriers, which corresponds to Fig. 4b.

CN, WO3 and composites are all n-type semiconductors as determined by Mott-Schottky curves ( Fig. 5a and Fig. S3). The flat band potential (E fb) of n-type semiconductors is approximately equal to the conduction band potential (E CB) [56]. The E fb of CN is – 1.02 eV vs. Ag/AgCl, and the E fb of WO3 is − 0.37 eV vs. Ag/AgCl. It can be converted into the normal hydrogen electrode (NHE) according to the following formula [57]:
(1)

NHE
= E
vs.Ag/AgCl
+ 0.059 × pH +
E
∅
( Ag / Ag Cl )
Where E θ (Ag/AgCl) is 0.197 eV and E(vs.Ag/AgCl) is the measured E fb. It can be obtained that the E CB of CN and WO3 are − 0.82 eV and − 0.17 eV, respectively. The conduction band position of the composite is closer to the center than that of CN. The slope of the linear part of Mott-Schottky curves is inversely proportional to the carrier density [58]. Therefore, the carrier density relationship is: 0.6CCW > 0.6CW > CN > WO3.

The band gap energy of the semiconductor can be obtained by UV-Vis DRS (Fig. 5b), and the calculation formula is as follows [59]:
(2)
αhν
1 / n
= A
hν −
E g
where α, h, ν and A are absorption coefficient, Planck's constant, frequency of the photon, and the constant, respectively. For the direct bandgap semiconductors, the value of n is 1/2, and for the indirect bandgap semiconductors CN and WO3, the value of n is 2. The bandgap of the photocatalysts can be obtained more accurately by the Tauc method (single semiconductors) and baseline method (composite catalysts), respectively [60]. The band gap of the composite material is between CN (2.81 eV) and WO3 (2.62 eV) according to Fig. 5b. Since the construction of heterojunctions will cause different semiconductors to form a unified fermi level, and semiconductors with different band gaps will affect the location of the unified fermi level. The barrier effect between WO3 and CN is reduced, thereby decreasing the band gap [58]. The UCPL property of the carbon quantum dots broadens the light absorption range and further reduces the band gap energy [61]. The valence band energy (E VB) of semiconductor can be calculated according to Eq. (3) [62].
(3)

=
E CB
+
E g

The E VB of CN and WO3 were measured by VB-XPS (Fig. S4) to further identify the veracity of the calculated semiconductor valence band. E VB, NHE can be calculated by the following formula [63]:
(4)

VB , NHE
= φ +
E
VB , XPS
− 4.44
where φ is the instrument work function (4.5 eV), and the resulting band structures of each semiconductor are listed in Table S3. The conduction band bottom (CBM) and valence band top (VBM) of the composites are closer to the center than CN and WO3, which is more conducive to the generation of photogenerated carriers [51].

Possible charge transfer mechanisms are proposed based on the above characterization results. Since the conduction band of WO3 is lower than the O2/·O2 - potential, the electron transfer mechanism of the conventional type-II heterojunction ( Fig. 6a) cannot explain the presence of ·O2 -. The Z-scheme electron transfer mechanism (Fig. 6b) can well prove this. The catalyst was excited under light to produce holes and electrons, and subsequently CQDs, an electronic mediator, facilitated the transfer of electrons from the CB of WO3 (E CB = −0.17 eV) to the VB of CN (E VB = 0.82 eV). CQDs can form an electron trap effect, and its oxygen-containing functional groups are combined with the semiconductors to reduce the internal resistance of the photocatalyst, thereby promoting charge transfer and separation [64]. The work function of CN and WO3 is calculated to further defined the charge transfer mechanism. The calculation results of CN (002) and WO3 (001) are displayed in Fig. 6c and Fig. 6d. Furthermore, Fig. S5 is the structural views of CN and WO3. The work function of CN is 4.803 eV, which is much smaller than that of WO3 (5.823 eV). The difference in the work function affects the flow of electrons from CN to WO3, which forms a unified Fermi energy level [30]. When the heterojunction is formed, the electrons in CN are spontaneously transferred to the WO3 surface, resulting in a built-in electric field at the interface. This promotes the complexation of the weakly reducing electrons in CN with the weakly oxidizing holes in WO3, which in turn leaves behind more strongly reducing electrons and more strongly oxidizing holes [65].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photocatalytic reaction of lignin model compounds
ce:section.ce:para:
The C-C bond cleavage was explored using 2-Phenoxy-1-phenylethanol (PP-ol) as the substrate. The effects of catalyst, reaction time, atmosphere and solvent on the cracking efficiency of PP-ol were compared shown in Fig. 7. And the reaction path, the mass spectra of the substrate and the product are shown in Fig. S6. The calculation formulas of the yield of the product, the conversion of the substrate and the selectivity of the C-C bond are as follows [53]:
(4)
Yield =
molar amount of product molar amount of substrate input
× 100
%
(5)
Conversion =
molar amount of substrate consumed molar amount of substrate input
× 100
%
(6)
C − C bond cleavage selectivity = 1 −
molar amount of PP − one
total molar amount of all products
× 100
%

Blank control experiments showed that PP-ol did not convert under the conditions of no catalyst in the darkness. It can be seen that both catalyst and light are necessary conditions for the reaction. Firstly, the effects of different catalysts on the photocatalytic conversion of PP-ol were explored at room temperature. Fig. 7a and Fig. S7 exhibit the reaction effects of different catalysts. The catalytic efficiency of ternary photocatalyst has been significantly improved compared with single catalyst and binary photocatalyst, which demonstrates that the construction of heterojunction can reduce the recombination rate of photocatalytic photogenerated carriers. Compared with single catalyst and binary photocatalyst, the catalytic efficiency of ternary photocatalyst has been significantly improved. It indicates that the construction of heterojunction can reduce the recombination rate of photocatalytic photogenerated carriers. The content of CN and WO3 in the composite material affects its photocatalytic efficiency. The ternary photocatalysts incorporating carbon quantum dots have better photocatalytic activity for two reasons. On one hand, the CQDs broaden the light absorption range and improve the utilization of visible light by the photocatalysts. On the other hand, the moderate amount of carbon quantum dots transfers the photogenerated electrons to the conduction band of the catalysts. However, excess carbon quantum dots participate in the competition of light absorption and reduce the photocatalytic activity [35]. It can be seen clearly from the illustration that 0.6CCW is the most suitable catalyst.

Multiple sets of experiments were performed at 30 min intervals under the same conditions (Fig. 7b) to investigate the effect of reaction time on substrate conversion, and some of the liquid phase diagrams are displayed in Fig. S8. Under the condition of 1.5 h reaction time, the conversion of PP-ol was > 99 %, the C-C bond selectivity was > 99 %, the yield of phenyl formate was 28.25 %, the total yield of benzaldehyde and benzoic acid reached to 83.92 %. With the extension of time, benzaldehyde was further transformed into benzoic acid, and the total yield of the product decreased.

The atmosphere has a substantial effect on the photocatalytic reaction (Fig. 7c). The substrate conversion under N2 atmosphere was only 54.24 % and the product yield was also low, and the by-product (PP-one) was not further converted. It suggests that the presence of oxygen is necessary for the production of ·O2 -, which in turn is an indispensable intermediate for C-C bond cleavage. Excess oxygen led to further conversion of the products, with benzoic acid yielding 50.96 %, but benzaldehyde yield decreased to 32.03 %.

Choosing the appropriate solvent is an important part to improve the substrate conversion rate and product yield. Table. S4 lists the effects of different solvents on the photocatalytic conversion of PP-ol. The conversion of PP-ol was only 30.3 % under the condition of ethanol as solvent. In contrast, under the condition of using chloroform as solvent, although the substrate conversion reached to 98.92 %, the total yield of benzaldehyde and benzoic acid was only 34.38 %, and PP-one reached to 29.97 %. Interestingly, under the condition of toluene as solvent, the conversion of PP-ol was only 15.79 %, but the benzaldehyde yield reached to 140.35 %, which was attributed to the catalytic oxidation of toluene to benzaldehyde by the photocatalyst under the light condition [66]. Acetonitrile has excellent solvency properties as a low toxicity solvent with satisfactory stability under visible light [67]. Acetonitrile, an aprotic solvent, has low adsorption to the substrate, which helps the catalyst to adsorb the substrate. Furthermore, high adsorption of the product by acetonitrile promotes the reaction [68]. The related researches on photocatalytic C-C bond cleavage of lignin is listed in Table S5.

The recyclability is also a crucial indicator to evaluate the performance of the catalyst. The catalyst was subjected to four cycles of experiments (Fig. 7d). The substrate conversion remained above 98 % after four cycle tests, and the total yield of benzaldehyde and benzoic acid could still reach about 65 %. Fig. S9 is the XRD and FTIR spectra of the catalyst before and after the reaction. The structure and morphology of the catalyst did not change significantly after the cyclic reaction compared with the fresh catalyst, which confirmed that the catalyst had good stability.

In order to further investigate the cleavage mechanism of the C-C bond, multiple sets of control experiments and free radical trapping experiments were performed. And the relevant radicals were analyzed by electron spin resonance (ESR). Scheme 1 explains the conversion of benzaldehyde, benzoic acid, phenyl formate and PP-one under the same reaction conditions. Benzaldehyde, as the main product, can be converted furtherly to benzoic acid with the yield of 54.4 %, which demonstrates the origin of benzoic acid. Nevertheless, benzoic acid and phenyl formate were not further oxidized to smaller molecules. In addition, as for the by-product (PP-one), it can be further evolved into benzaldehyde, benzoic acid and phenyl formate.

The active substances in the reaction process were verified by free radical capture experiments. Fig. 8a depicts the conversion and product yields of the substrates with the addition of·O2 - captor (PBQ), 1O2 captor (Trp), h + captor (EDTA-2Na), e
- captor (AgNO3), and free-radical-captor (DMPO). The total yield of benzoic acid and benzaldehyde decreased to 47.69 % after the addition of PBQ, which demonstrates that ·O2 - is an important active substance to promote the reaction. Whereas, the substrate conversion reached more than 95 %, which is possibly due to the fact that PBQ promoted the conversion of PP-ol [24]. It can also be revealed by Fig. 8b that ·O2 -has a strong ESR signal. The product yield did not change significantly after adding Trp, therefore the ESR signal peak intensity of 1O2 became very weak, which signifies that 1O2 has little effect on the reaction process.

The lack of water in the system makes it difficult to generate ·OH, so its ESR signal is very weak. Moreover, after the addition of EDTA-2Na, the total yield of the product decreased significantly, and the yield of phenyl formate was only 1.85 %, which might be related to the side reaction of breaking the C-O bond [69]. Besides, since e
- is an essential element for the generation of ·O2 -, the addition of AgNO3 also result in an enormously decrease in the yield of the products. After the addition of DMPO, the active substance (C β radical) was removed, which made the reaction difficult to carry out [16]. The strong ESR signal of C β radical in Fig. 8c also proves that it is the main intermediate of the reaction.

The possible reaction mechanism was proposed as follow based on the above research results. The mechanism diagram is displayed in Scheme 2. Photocatalysts generate photogenerated electrons and holes under light conditions. As an electronic medium, CQDs provide an electron transport channel for the photogenerated electrons in the conduction band of WO3, which promotes its recombination with the holes in the CN valence band, thus leaving h + with stronger oxidation and e
- with stronger reducibility [70]. For the side reaction, PP-ol is oxidized to PP-one by h +, but the C α -C β with high dissociation energy in PP-one is difficult to be further cleaved [71]. For the main reaction, h + drive the dissociation of C β -H to generate C β radical [72]. And the photogenerated electrons in the conduction band of CN can combine with O2 to generate ·O2 -, which subsequently couples with C β radical to form a six-membered ring transition state [73]. Whereafter, elec tron transfer facilitates the cleavage of the C α -C β bond, thereby shedding one molecule of water to produce benzaldehyde and phenyl formate. And benzaldehyde can be further partially oxidized to benzoic acid in the presence of oxygen [74].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Exploring the universality and practicality of photocatalytic methods
ce:section.ce:para: Photocatalytic experiments were carried on a variety of β-O-4 lignin model compounds under identical conditions to explore the general applicability of photocatalysts. It is obvious from Scheme 3 that the substrates all have a conversion of more than 90 % with good selectivity.

The universality of photocatalysts was explored by performing photocatalytic reactions on commercial lignin (alkali lignin) and natural lignin (grass lignin, pine lignin, birch lignin). The depolymerization effect of lignin was assessed by gas chromatography-mass spectrometer (GC–MS), gel permeation chromatography (GPC) and FTIR.

The products after lignin reaction detected by GC-MS are listed in Table S6-S9. The products of the alkali lignin reaction are mainly monocyclic aromatic hydrocarbons. One of the products, vanillin, a widely used high value-added chemical has the highest content [59]. A variety of monocyclic aromatic hydrocarbons were presented in the grass lignin after photocatalytic depolymerization. In the aromatic hydrocardons, 2-Methoxyresorcinol, an intermediate for the synthesis of important chemicals, accounted for 41.83 %. Pine lignin, one of the three natural lignans, is a soft lignin with a wider product distribution. Among them, methyl dehydroabietate with the highest content is used as plasticizer and pharmaceutical field [75]. Extracted birch lignin may contain unremoved cellulose and hemicellulose, which affects the photocatalytic effect and results in less product [76]. 3-hydroxy-3-phenylpropionic acid, one of the interested main products, is an intermediate in the synthesis of other organic compounds. It is used as an antioxidant and anti-inflammatory agent in the pharmaceutical field and also as a food additive with antioxidant and preservative properties.

The change of molecular weight of lignin was analyzed by GPC. The GPC chromatogram is presented in Fig. S10 and the average molecular weight distribution is listed in Table S9. Due to the large amounts of macromolecular substances in lignin raw materials, the retention time is earlier than that of lignin products. This certifies that the structure of lignin has been obviously broken [77]. The heavy average molecular weight (Mw) of the product is much smaller than that of the raw lignin and the product has a narrower average molecular weight distribution. This further corroborates that lignin has undergone obvious depolymerization. The results of GPC analysis corresponded to the results of GC-MS.

The changes in lignin functional groups were analyzed by FTIR as displayed in Fig. S11 and Table S11. Compared with the raw lignin, the absorption peak intensity of some C-O bonds in the product decreased significantly, and even some absorption peaks disappeared, which was caused by the breakage of the main connecting bonds of lignin. Besides, some new absorption peaks appeared, which corresponds to the C-H bond bending vibration of the partially substituted benzene ring and the C-O stretching vibration of the alcohol ketone. The absorption peak in the range of 1510 cm−1-1614 cm−1 is related to the skeleton vibration of the benzene ring without significant change. It can be known that the products are mainly aromatic compounds combined with the GC-MS analysis, which provides a strategy for the high value utilization of lignin.

In summary, the Z-scheme heterojunction photocatalyst 0.6CCW was prepared by ultrasonic assisted wet impregnation method. For the β-O-4 model compound, under a short time interval (1.5 h), the higher conversion (> 99 %) and C-C bond selectivity (> 99 %) as well as the total yield of benzaldehyde and benzoic acid (83.92 %) were achieved than the single catalyst, and the photocatalyst also had satisfactory depolymerization effect on the four lignin simples. The ESR and work function results corroborate that the more positive valence band of WO3 enables the generation of more oxidizing holes to realize the generation of C β radicals, while the generation of ·O2 - from the electrons in the CN conduction band with O2 accelerates the generation of the product. CQDs as electronic media not only broaden the absorption range of photocatalysts, but also promote the transport of photogenerated carriers and improve the photocatalyst activity. This study provides solutions for the construction of visible photocatalysts to promote lignin conversion.

Zhijie Jiang: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Xianzhe Zhang: Supervision, Formal analysis. Xutang Liu: Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Mei Shi: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Conceptualization. Mingyu Cui: Methodology.
